title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Theoretical Analysis of the Label Trick in Graph Neural Networks

## Summary

This paper provides a theoretical analysis of the "label trick," a widely-adopted heuristic in graph neural networks that randomly partitions training labels into two sets—one concatenated with node features as input, and the other used for supervision. The authors prove that under linear assumptions (SGC-like models) and MSE loss, the stochastic label trick reduces to a deterministic objective comprising: (1) a data-fitting term using self-excluded label propagation that prevents label leakage, and (2) a graph-dependent regularization term that adapts to graph structure and connectivity. The paper extends these results to nonlinear models and categorical cross-entropy loss, then proposes three novel applications: trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick variants for linear GNNs, and trainable Correct and Smooth. Experiments on node classification benchmarks are presented to validate the theoretical insights.

## Strengths

1. **Addresses a significant theoretical gap**: The label trick is demonstrably important—it appears in the top 10 results on the OGB leaderboard and top 3 results from the KDD Cup 2021 MAG240M challenge. However, despite this widespread empirical success, the technique has lacked formal theoretical justification. This paper fills that gap rigorously.

2. **Rigorous mathematical contributions**: Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 provide elegant, closed-form decompositions of the stochastic objective into interpretable components. The progression from simplified cases (linear models without features in Section 3.1) through to general feature-label combinations (Section 3.3) is pedagogically sound. The proofs appear technically sound with detailed derivations in the appendix.

3. **Clear mechanistic insights**: Remarks 1-5 effectively explain *why* the label trick works. Remark 1 shows how self-excluded propagation naturally resolves label leakage, Remark 2 establishes train-test consistency, and Remarks 4-5 characterize how the graph-dependent regularization term Γ adapts to graph structure and scales with graph size. These insights move beyond empirical observation to provide genuine understanding.

4. **Well-motivated practical extensions**: The three use cases (trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick for SGC/SIGN/TWIRLS, trainable C&S) follow naturally from the theoretical analysis and demonstrate that the framework has broader applicability beyond the original label trick formulation.

5. **Appropriate experimental design**: Rather than attempting to prove superiority over baselines (which would be redundant given prior work), the experiments appropriately focus on validating theoretical predictions and demonstrating the proposed extensions.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Significant theory-practice gap**: The main theoretical results (Theorems 1-2) assume linear propagation models, yet most practical GNNs are nonlinear with multiple layers. While Theorem 3 extends the analysis to nonlinear GNNs, it only characterizes the boundary cases where α→0 or α→1, leaving the practical regime with intermediate α values poorly understood theoretically. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not fully resolve it. For the gap between theory (MSE loss) and practice (cross-entropy classification), Theorem 2 provides only an upper bound via Jensen's inequality. The tightness of this bound is not discussed, and the practical relevance of MSE-based analysis for classification tasks remains unclear.

2. **Weak empirical validation of proposed applications**: The paper's stated goal is to "showcase the broader application scenarios" (Sec 4), positioning these as the main empirical contribution. However, examining Tables 1-4 carefully reveals that the label trick provides only marginal or comparable performance to baselines in most scenarios. In many cases, the improvements fall within the standard deviation across ten independent runs. For instance, Table 3 shows minimal gains on Cora-full (66.44→67.40), and the paper itself notes that the label trick provides minimal benefit when training accuracy is already near 100%. This severely limits the practical applicability of the proposed extensions. The deterministic label trick variant shows particularly mixed results, with no improvement on Cora-full, raising questions about its generality.

3. **Incomplete analysis of the nonlinear case**: Theorem 3's requirement that E[∑_{i∈D_out} ℓ(y_i, f_GNN[X,Y^in;W]_i)] be bounded for all D_out is somewhat opaque, and the practical implications of this condition are not clearly discussed. The theory only guarantees that test-node predictions during training match inference predictions when α→1, which may not be the practical regime of interest. For general nonlinear models at intermediate α, the paper provides no explicit theoretical characterization, forcing practitioners to rely entirely on empirical validation.

4. **Restrictive and unjustified assumptions**: The analysis assumes a specific propagation matrix form and does not address heterophily, directed graphs, or mini-batching—all of which are practically important. The self-excluded simplification (|D_out|=1) used for theoretical analysis differs from the stochastic version with arbitrary |D_out|, and while the paper presents them as equivalent, experiments show they behave differently (Figure 1). The practical relevance of the simplified case deserves more thorough validation. Additionally, the paper does not discuss computational costs: computing (P-C) and Γ may be expensive for very large graphs.

5. **Unclear role and necessity of the label trick**: While the paper demonstrates formal properties of the label trick, it does not establish *why* the label trick is necessary. What desirable effects—faster convergence, more robust training, better generalization—make it preferable to alternatives? The paper makes only a shallow remark about top leaderboard results without conducting ablation studies comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there simpler alternatives (e.g., label smoothing, mixup-based approaches) that achieve similar effects? This fundamental question remains unanswered.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Limited direct empirical validation of theoretical predictions**: While the paper reports experimental improvements, it does not directly validate key theoretical predictions. For example, the regularization effect predicted by Γ in Theorem 1 is only partially validated in Figure 2 and Appendix A.3. More direct empirical verification that the theoretical bounds hold in practice would strengthen the claims. Figure 1 would benefit from error bars or confidence intervals.

2. **Presentation and clarity issues**: 
   - The notation is sometimes dense (e.g., multiple mask matrices M^tr, M^in, M^out, M^te), and a notation table would improve readability.
   - The connection between the simplified one-vs-all case (Section 3.2) and the full stochastic version could be explained more intuitively earlier.
   - Section 4.2 on deterministic application to linear GNNs is dense; the formulation in (13) could be clearer.
   - Some experimental details are relegated to appendices, making reproducibility verification difficult.

3. **Incomplete ablations and sensitivity analysis**:
   - The splitting probability α requires tuning (Figure 1), but the paper provides limited guidance on how to select α in practice beyond grid search. The relationship between graph properties and optimal α deserves deeper investigation.
   - No systematic study of how sensitive results are to the choice of propagation matrix P, which is assumed to be given.
   - Missing ablations on how performance degrades with graph heterophily or other graph properties.
   - Table 3 uses "one specific set of hyperparameters" without tuning for label trick variants; this may underestimate the method's potential.

4. **Incomplete related work engagement**: Recent work on implicit regularization in GNNs and other methods for combining labels and features (e.g., APPNP, GCN-LPA, masked label prediction) are mentioned but not experimentally compared. The paper does not explain why the label trick works better than simpler alternatives.

5. **Statistical significance concerns**: Many improvements in Tables 1-4 are small (1-2% in many cases) with overlapping error bars, making it unclear whether gains are statistically significant. The paper acknowledges high variance but doesn't always clearly separate signal from noise.

6. **Reproducibility concerns**: Hyperparameter search ranges are provided, but the paper does not specify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each method or shared, which could bias comparisons. Code availability is not mentioned, and some implementation details (e.g., how P and C are computed for large graphs) are vague.

## Technical Issues

1. Theorem 2's bound uses Jensen's inequality, but the tightness of this bound in practice is not discussed.

2. The rescaling by 1/α in Section 3.3 to maintain consistent mean/variance is reasonable but not deeply justified. Why is this the right normalization rather than alternatives?

3. Remark 4 claims

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper provides rigorous theoretical justification for the label trick, a heuristic technique that randomly partitions training labels into two sets—one concatenated with node features as input and the other used for supervision—to improve semi-supervised node classification in graph neural networks. The authors prove that under linear propagation assumptions, the stochastic label trick reduces to a deterministic objective comprising two interpretable terms: (1) a data-fitting term using self-excluded label propagation that prevents label leakage, and (2) a graph-dependent regularization term that adapts to graph structure and connectivity. The paper extends these results to nonlinear models and proposes three novel applications: trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for linear GNNs (SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS), and trainable Correct and Smooth. Experiments on standard benchmarks validate the theoretical findings and demonstrate practical improvements.

## Strengths

1. **Addresses an important theoretical gap**: The label trick is widely adopted in top-performing GNN systems on major benchmarks (OGB leaderboard, KDD Cup) but has lacked formal justification. This work fills that gap with rigorous analysis, which is valuable for advancing theoretical understanding of practical techniques.

2. **Strong and well-presented theoretical contributions**: Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 elegantly decompose the stochastic label trick into interpretable components with clean, closed-form characterizations. The progression from simplified cases (linear models without features, |D_out|=1) to general settings (features, arbitrary |D_out|) is pedagogically sound. The mathematical derivations appear sound with detailed proofs provided in the appendix.

3. **Valuable theoretical insights**: Remarks 1-5 effectively communicate why the label trick works. Particularly insightful are: (i) how the label trick naturally resolves label leakage through self-excluded propagation (Remark 1), (ii) how it ensures consistent train-test predictions (Remark 2), and (iii) how it provides graph-adaptive regularization that scales with graph size and connectivity (Remarks 4-5). These insights are non-obvious and advance our understanding of the mechanism.

4. **Well-motivated practical extensions**: The three use cases (trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick for linear layers, trainable C&S) follow naturally from the theoretical analysis and demonstrate breadth of applicability. The deterministic simplification in particular is clever and practical, enabling efficient computation without random sampling.

5. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The paper includes experiments across multiple datasets (Cora-full, Pubmed, OGB-ArXiv, OGB-Products), proper reporting of standard deviations, ablations on the splitting probability α, and analysis of how training accuracy affects the label trick's utility. The experimental design appropriately focuses on validating theoretical predictions rather than claiming superiority over baselines.

## Weaknesses

1. **Significant gap between theory and practice**: The main theoretical results (Theorems 1-2) assume linear propagation models (SGC-like), yet most practical GNNs are deeply nonlinear. While Theorem 3 addresses nonlinear cases, it only characterizes the boundary cases (α→0, α→1), leaving the general nonlinear regime with intermediate α values poorly understood theoretically. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not fully resolve it. This is a fundamental constraint on the applicability of the theoretical insights to real-world deep GNN architectures.

2. **Restrictive assumptions limiting theoretical scope**: 
   - The analysis assumes MSE loss for closed-form results; categorical cross-entropy (the standard loss for classification) only receives an upper bound via Theorem 2. The tightness of this bound is not discussed, and the practical relevance of MSE-based analysis is therefore limited.
   - The self-excluded simplification (|D_out|=1) used for theoretical analysis differs from the stochastic version with arbitrary |D_out|. While this simplification is convenient for derivation, the practical relevance of this theoretical variant versus the original stochastic approach deserves deeper investigation.
   - The analysis does not address heterophilic graphs, directed graphs, or mini-batching, all of which are important in practice.

3. **Modest and sometimes marginal experimental improvements**: Many improvements in Tables 1-4 are small (e.g., 66.44→67.40 on Cora-full, ~1-2% gains in many cases) and fall within noise margins. Table 3 shows that the deterministic label trick provides no improvement on Cora-full, suggesting limited generality. The paper acknowledges high variance but does not always clearly separate signal from noise, and some comparisons use overlapping error bars, making statistical significance unclear.

4. **Limited practical applicability guidance**: The paper notes that the label trick provides minimal benefit when training accuracy is already near 100% (Appendix A.1), which limits its applicability to well-conditioned problems. However, there is limited guidance on how practitioners should select the splitting probability α beyond grid search, or how to determine a priori whether the label trick will help for a given problem. The relationship between graph properties and optimal α deserves deeper investigation.

5. **Incomplete analysis of nonlinear case**: Theorem 3's requirement that E[∑_{i∈D_out} ℓ(y_i, f_GNN[X,Y^in;W]_i)] be bounded for all D_out is somewhat opaque, and the practical implications of this condition are not clearly discussed. The claim that test-node predictions during training match inference predictions (Remark 2) only holds in the limit α→1, which may not be the practical regime of interest.

6. **Experimental validation focuses on practical improvements rather than theory verification**: While the experiments are comprehensive in scope, they focus primarily on demonstrating practical improvements rather than directly validating theoretical predictions. For instance, the regularization effect predicted by Γ in Theorem 1 is only partially validated in Figure 2 and Appendix A.3. More direct empirical verification of the theoretical bounds and characterizations would strengthen the claims.

7. **Missing comparisons and ablations**:
   - No systematic comparison against other hybrid label-feature propagation methods beyond C&S (e.g., APPNP, GCN-LPA are mentioned but not experimentally compared).
   - Limited comparison against recent strong baselines or alternative approaches for combining labels and features.
   - No analysis of how sensitive results are to the choice of propagation matrix P or performance degradation on heterophilic graphs.
   - Hyperparameter selection details are unclear; the paper does not specify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each method, which could bias comparisons.

8. **Presentation and clarity issues**:
   - The paper conflates the stochastic label trick (random splits each epoch) with the deterministic variant (|D_out|=1 always), presenting them as equivalent when experiments show they behave differently (Figure 1). This relationship deserves more explicit discussion.
   - Notation is sometimes dense (e.g., multiple mask matrices M_tr, M_out, M_in, M_te, multiple uses of P, C). A notation table would improve readability.
   - The connection between the simplified one-vs-all case (Section 3.2) and the full stochastic version could be explained more intuitively earlier in the paper.
   - Some experimental details are relegated to appendices, making reproducibility verification and the main narrative harder to follow.

## Technical Issues

1. **Theorem 2's bound for cross-entropy**: The use of Jensen's inequality provides an upper bound, but the tightness of this bound in practice is not discussed. Given that cross-entropy is the standard loss, tighter characterizations or empirical validation of the bound's usefulness would be valuable.

2. **Regularization term characterization**: The regularization factor Γ in Theorem 1 is defined implicitly through matrix operations. More intuition about when this regularization is beneficial, how its magnitude varies across different graph types, and whether it provides the "right" form of regularization (beyond mathematical derivation) would strengthen the analysis.

3. **Rescaling justification**: The rescaling by 1/α in Section 3.3 to maintain consistent mean/variance is reasonable but not deeply justified. Why is this the specific normalization choice?

4. **Computational complexity not discussed**: The paper does not discuss computational costs. Computing (P-C)Y^tr and Γ may be expensive for very large graphs, and this overhead should be characterized.

5. **Remark 4 precision**: The claim that Γ grows with graph size depends on whether edges grow proportionally with nodes. This statement could be more precise about the conditions under which this holds.

## Minor Issues

- Figure 1 would benefit from error bars or confidence intervals to better convey uncertainty.
- The paper claims the label trick is "foundational" to top OGB submissions but provides limited quantitative evidence beyond anecdotal references.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Theoretical Analysis of the Label Trick in Graph Neural Networks

## Summary

This paper provides theoretical justification for the "label trick," a widely-adopted heuristic in graph neural networks where training labels are randomly partitioned and concatenated with node features as input. Under simplifying assumptions (linear propagation, MSE loss), the authors prove that the stochastic label trick reduces to a deterministic objective comprising two interpretable terms: (1) a data-fitting term using self-excluded label propagation that prevents label leakage, and (2) a graph-dependent regularization term that adapts to graph structure and size. The paper extends these results to nonlinear models and proposes three applications: trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for linear GNNs (SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS), and trainable Correct & Smooth. Experiments on standard benchmarks validate the theoretical insights.

## Strengths

**(+) Addresses an important theoretical gap.** The paper tackles a fundamental problem: the label trick has achieved widespread empirical success on major benchmarks (OGB leaderboard, KDD Cup) yet has lacked formal theoretical justification. Providing rigorous analysis of why this heuristic works is valuable for the community and connects classical label propagation methods with modern message-passing neural networks.

**(+) Strong theoretical contributions with clear intuition.** Theorem 1 and Corollary 1 elegantly decompose the stochastic label trick into interpretable components. The key insight—that the label trick naturally resolves label leakage through self-excluded propagation while providing graph-adaptive regularization—is well-articulated. Remarks 1-5 effectively communicate why the method works: preventing label leakage (Remark 1), maintaining train-test consistency (Remark 2), and providing regularization that scales with graph size and connectivity (Remarks 3-5). The progression from simplified cases (linear models without features) to general settings is pedagogically sound.

**(+) Practical extensions well-motivated by theory.** The three proposed applications (trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick for linear GNNs, trainable C&S) naturally follow from the theoretical analysis. These extensions demonstrate that the theoretical framework has broader applicability beyond explaining existing methods.

**(+) The deterministic simplification is clever and practical.** Equation (10) makes intuitive sense: keeping only off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix avoids the undesirable identity mapping solution while maintaining the benefits of label propagation. This simplification is both theoretically motivated and computationally efficient.

**(+) Comprehensive experimental validation across multiple datasets.** The experiments span diverse settings (Cora-full, Pubmed, OGB-ArXiv, OGB-Products) with proper reporting of standard deviations, ablations on splitting probability α, and analysis of how training accuracy affects the label trick's benefits.

## Major Weaknesses

**(–) Significant gap between theory and practice.** The main theoretical results (Theorems 1-2) assume either linear propagation models (SGC-like) or MSE loss. While Theorem 3 extends to nonlinear GNNs, it only characterizes the boundary cases (α→0, α→1), leaving the practical regime with intermediate α values poorly understood theoretically. Most practical GNNs are nonlinear and use categorical cross-entropy loss, yet the analysis provides only an upper bound (Theorem 2) rather than tight characterization. This fundamental limitation undermines the applicability of the theoretical insights to real-world scenarios.

**(–) Restrictive and sometimes unclear assumptions.** The analysis assumes a specific propagation matrix form and does not address heterophily, directed graphs, or mini-batching (standard in practice). More critically, the self-excluded simplification (|D_out|=1) used for theoretical analysis differs from the stochastic version with arbitrary |D_out|, yet the practical relevance of this simplification is not thoroughly validated. The paper conflates the stochastic label trick (random splits each epoch) with the deterministic variant, but experiments show they behave differently (Figure 1), deserving more discussion.

**(–) Equation (3) and the trainable smoothing step lack clear intuition.** While the paper motivates the label trick well, the intuition behind expressing the column space of Y_tr by post-multiplying with weight matrix W is not adequately explained. Why is this the appropriate parameterization? Similar concerns apply to the trainable smoothing step in Correct & Smooth. This is a significant gap given that the paper has theoretical flavor and should justify key design choices.

**(–) Modest and sometimes inconclusive experimental improvements.** The improvements in Tables 1, 3, and 4 are often small (1-2% in many cases) with overlapping error bars, making it unclear whether gains are statistically significant. For instance, Table 3 shows Cora-full with minimal improvement (66.44→67.40), and the paper acknowledges that benefits primarily manifest when training accuracy is low. This substantially limits the method's applicability. Furthermore, the deterministic label trick variant shows mixed results across datasets, questioning its generality.

**(–) Limited direct empirical validation of theoretical predictions.** While the paper demonstrates practical improvements, it focuses less on directly validating theoretical claims. For example, the regularization effect predicted by the term ||ΓW_y||² in Theorem 1 is only partially validated in Figure 2 and Appendix A.3. More rigorous empirical verification of the theoretical bounds and decomposition would strengthen the claims. The paper would benefit from experiments specifically designed to test whether the theoretical characterization holds in practice.

**(–) Incomplete analysis of the nonlinear case.** Theorem 3's requirement that E[∑_{i∈D_out} ℓ(y_i, f_GNN[X,Y^in;W]_i)] be bounded for all D_out is opaque, and the practical implications are not clearly discussed. The condition only guarantees that test predictions during training match inference in the limit α→1, which may not be the practical regime. No explicit characterization exists for general α and nonlinear models.

**(–) Missing comparisons with related work and recent baselines.** The paper does not adequately compare against other works that combine label and feature propagation (e.g., Jia & Benson 2021, APPNP, GCN-LPA), even experimentally. Recent strong baselines (GraphSAINT, DropMessage) and semi-supervised methods beyond C&S are absent. Additionally, the paper does not discuss why the label trick works better than simpler alternatives like data augmentation or mixup. Given the paper's theoretical flavor, comparison with other works analyzing label and feature propagation would be valuable.

## Minor Weaknesses

**(–) Clarity and presentation issues.**

- Some equations are unclear or difficult to parse. Equation (5) and the transition from the second-last to final term needs elaboration. Given that D_out has cardinality 1 in the simplification, should the expectation not simplify to (1/n)∑_{i∈D_tr} ℓ(y_i, [P(Y_tr - Y_i W]_i)? The subscript notation E_{splits} is vague and should be clarified.

- The paper is notionally dense, particularly with excessive use of subscripts (multiple mask matrices M^tr, M^out, M^in, M^te). A notation table would help readers. The stream of remarks, while insightful, makes the text harder to follow.

- Section 4.1 is unclear and lacks detail. How is this procedure trained? What is the final model—is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix? References to other papers learning or modifying propagation matrices should be provided.

- The connection between the self-excluded simplification (Section 3.2) and the full stochastic version could be explained more intuitively earlier in the paper.

**(–) Missing or incomplete details.**

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot of Figure 1? Which GNN is used in Section 5.3? This is unspecified.

- In Remark 4, if regularization is shut off, why is there "no chance for overfitting"? This claim needs justification.

- If the derivation shows that the deterministic case can be decomposed with a regularization term, is this used for results in Table 3? If not (as it does not appear to be mentioned), why not?

- Hyperparameter selection details: The splitting probability α requires tuning (Figure 1), but limited guidance is provided on how to select α in practice beyond grid search. The relationship between graph properties and optimal α deserves deeper investigation.

- Reproducibility concerns: The paper does not specify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each method or shared, which could bias comparisons. Code availability is not mentioned,
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Understanding the Label Trick in Graph-Based Semi-Supervised Learning

## Summary

This paper investigates the "label trick," a widely-adopted heuristic in graph neural networks that concatenates randomly-selected training labels with node features as GNN inputs for semi-supervised node classification. Despite its empirical success on major benchmarks (OGB leaderboard, KDD Cup), the label trick has lacked formal theoretical justification. The authors prove that under linear propagation assumptions, the stochastic label trick reduces to a deterministic objective comprising two terms: (1) a data-fitting term using self-excluded label propagation that prevents label leakage, and (2) a graph-dependent regularization term that adapts to graph structure and size. The paper extends these results to nonlinear models, proposes three novel applications (trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for linear GNNs, and trainable Correct and Smooth), and validates findings experimentally.

## Strengths

1. **Important theoretical contribution addressing a practical gap**: The paper fills a significant void by providing formal justification for a heuristic that has achieved widespread empirical success. It is commendable to take a step back and understand what might appear to be "wizard engineering," demonstrating that the label trick can be understood through simple models. This work will be valuable for practitioners seeking to understand why the label trick works.

2. **Rigorous mathematical analysis with clear insights**: Theorems 1 and Corollary 1 provide clean, interpretable decompositions of the stochastic objective into data-fitting and regularization terms. The progression from simplified cases (linear models without features) to general settings (full feature-label combinations) is pedagogically sound. Remarks 1-5 effectively communicate key insights: the label trick prevents label leakage through self-excluded propagation, maintains train-test consistency, and provides graph-adaptive regularization that scales with problem size and connectivity.

3. **Practical extensions well-motivated by theory**: The three use cases (trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick for SGC/SIGN/TWIRLS, trainable Correct and Smooth) follow naturally from the theoretical analysis and demonstrate the framework's broader applicability. The deterministic simplification is particularly clever, enabling efficient computation without random sampling.

4. **Relatively clear exposition**: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow, with detailed proofs in the appendix supporting the main claims.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Substantial gap between theory and practice**: The main theoretical results (Theorems 1-2) assume linearity or MSE loss, neither of which holds for practical deep GNNs with cross-entropy loss. While Theorem 3 extends to nonlinear GNNs, it only characterizes the boundary cases (α→0 or α→1), leaving the practical intermediate regime poorly understood theoretically. The extension to categorical cross-entropy (Theorem 2) provides only an upper bound via Jensen's inequality, whose tightness is not discussed. This severely limits the practical relevance of the theoretical insights for real-world nonlinear networks.

2. **Limited rigor and incomplete theoretical justification**: The analysis relies on restrictive assumptions that are not always clearly stated or justified:
   - The self-excluded simplification (|D_out|=1) is used for theoretical convenience but differs from the stochastic version with arbitrary |D_out|; the practical equivalence of these variants is not thoroughly validated (Figure 1 shows they behave differently).
   - The assumption of a specific propagation matrix form does not address heterophilic graphs or directed graphs.
   - No treatment of mini-batching, which is standard in practice.
   - The paper does not rigorously explain why the label trick is superior to other approaches, such as GNN with Laplacian regularization (Pfau et al., Cabannes et al.) or MixUp-based regularization (Lopez-Paz et al.), leaving a critical gap in the comparative analysis.

3. **Weak experimental validation of theoretical claims**: 
   - Improvements in Tables 1, 3, and 4 are often modest (1-2% in many cases) with overlapping error bars, making statistical significance unclear. The paper acknowledges high variance but does not consistently separate signal from noise.
   - The label trick primarily helps when training accuracy is low (Appendix A.1), limiting applicability to well-conditioned problems. For instance, on Cora-full with near-100% training accuracy, the label trick provides minimal benefit, suggesting limited generality.
   - Experiments focus more on demonstrating practical improvements than directly validating theoretical predictions. The regularization effect predicted by Γ in Theorem 1 is only partially validated in Figure 2 and Appendix A.3; more direct empirical verification of the theoretical bounds would strengthen the claims.
   - Missing comparisons with recent strong baselines (e.g., GraphSAINT, DropMessage) and other hybrid label-feature propagation methods (APPNP, GCN-LPA are mentioned but not experimentally compared).

4. **Insufficient guidance for practitioners**: The paper does not provide clear answers to fundamental practical questions:
   - How should practitioners select the splitting probability α without access to validation data? Figure 1 shows α requires tuning, but only grid search is discussed.
   - When should the label trick be expected to help or hurt performance?
   - What is the computational overhead of computing P and C for very large graphs?
   - Why is the deterministic variant (|D_out|=1) not used in practice if theory suggests equivalence?
   These gaps limit the practical impact of the theoretical framework.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Presentation and clarity issues**:
   - The paper conflates the stochastic label trick (random splits each epoch) with the deterministic variant (|D_out|=1 always), presenting them as equivalent when experiments demonstrate they behave differently. This relationship deserves more careful treatment.
   - Notation is sometimes dense (multiple mask matrices M_tr, M_out, M_in, M_te; multiple uses of P, C). A notation table would improve readability.
   - Section 4.2 on deterministic application to linear GNNs is dense; formulation in Eq. (13) could be clearer.
   - At the outset (page 1, line 4), the introduction conflates two different issues: that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph versus that samples are i.i.d. These are distinct problems, and discussing both adds confusion rather than clarity.
   - The formalism could be more rigorous. For instance, in the abstract, the "identity mapping" from inputs in ℝ^d to outputs in ℝ^c is not well-defined; this appears to conflate the transductive setting with feature propagation, which should be clarified.

2. **Technical presentation concerns**:
   - Equation (1) appears to penalize F toward zero on test data (using Pythagoras). The correct formulation should use F_tr; moreover, the fact that Y = 0 is informative (e.g., as logits) and that pushing F toward 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
   - The notation F ∈ ℝ^{n×c} should be explicitly stated, and it should be noted that minimizing the objective can be done independently for each coordinate of Y ∈ ℝ^c if there are no constraints on F.
   - On page 3, there is a mistake with P ≈ PY; the approximation can be written as an equality with an infinite series since λS is contracting.
   - The definition of P as a "propagation matrix" in Eq. (3) should be clarified—how is P fixed here?

3. **Incomplete related work engagement**:
   - Recent work on implicit regularization in GNNs is not discussed.
   - The paper does not compare against or discuss masked label prediction methods (e.g., Shi et al., 2021, which is cited but not experimentally compared).
   - The paper does not explain why the label trick works better than simpler alternatives like data augmentation or mixup.

4. **Reproducibility concerns**:
   - The paper does not specify whether hyperparameters were tuned separately for each method or shared, which could bias comparisons.
   - Code availability is not mentioned.
   - Some implementation details (e.g., how P and C are computed for large graphs) are vague.
   - Table 3 uses "one specific set of hyperparameters" without tuning for label trick variants, potentially underestimating the method's potential.

5. **Theoretical gaps**:
   - The regularization term Γ in Eq. (8) is defined implicitly; its magnitude and behavior across different graph types are not thoroughly analyzed. Why is the form ||ΓW_y||²_F the "right" regularization beyond mathematical derivation?
   - Remark 4
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.

Main Review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Summary Of The Review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
