  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.

Main Review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.
  - S2: The efficiency of the proposed method is
    investigated very clearly in Section 4.3. It seems
    considerably more efficient than many other
    learning-based DA methods.
  - S3: The patch-wise data augmentation is a powerful idea
    which can spark a large body of interesting follow-up
    research.


## Limitations

  - L1: While this is not the main focus of the paper, the
    empirical improvement over RL baselines like Fast
    AutoAugment and non-RL baselines like RandAugment is
    relatively small.
  - L2: (Minor) While multiple datasets are used for
    evaluation, only two Resnet variants are investigated
    for ImageNet. It would be nice to see an evaluation of
    the proposed data augmentation procedure on other NN
    architectures as well on ImageNet, but I also
    understand that the computational costs of this can be
    quite large, so it may not always be possible.
  - L3: (Minor) The agents rely on a pre-trained ResNet to
    produce features to be used as observations.


## Suggestions (Per-Section)

### Related Work

  - This is minor, but it may be worth briefly mentioning
    [hong2019patch]. While their work doesn't do patch
    level data augmentation, and instead "patches" objects
    from an existing bank onto training samples, it still
    has connections to things like CutMix that make it
    relevant.
  - Some recent papers on automatic augmentation are
    missing and would be worth discussing. This includes
    [DADA], [DDAS], [hendrycks2019augmix] and
    [mueller2021trivialAugment]. In particular, Mueller and
    Hutter's paper highlights an interesting limitation of
    their otherwise robust baseline, which is that it
    doesn't work too well for object detection, an area
    where the current method may have the upper hand!
  - Similarly, there are some further papers, such as […]
    that perform a kind of "local" (though not strictly
    patch-based) data augmentation by "pasting in" objects
    into the 3D point cloud.


### Method

  - "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL)
    algorithm to …" => "algorithms"
  - "In MARL configuration" => "In the MARL configuration"
  - Towards the end of Section 3.2, it may make more sense
    to replace the "Until all patches" to "Once all
    patches…" or maybe "After".
  - Q1: Can an agent output more than one augmentation for
    a patch? Would it be possible for a patch to, e.g., get
    rotated AND colorized?
  - Q2: Given the more "local" nature of this augmentation
    method, have you considered evaluating it on more
    "local" tasks, such as object detection and semantic
    segmentation. Perhaps it could make a bigger impact
    there.
  - Q3: Given that the parameters of actor networks of all
    agents are shared (see above Eq. (5)), does that mean
    that each actor's policy is identical, and the only
    difference between the actors is what observation each
    one gets (the local patch features)?


### Experiments

  - At the start of Section 4.1, "Exactly, we focus" =>
    "Specifically, we focus"
  - In "Policy Visualization", "important levels" ->
    "importance levels"
  - In Section 4.3 I would say the "main methods for
    reducing the computational cost", since what you next
    describe are methods for reducing the cost, not what
    one would typically call reasons.
  - Table 3 is a little hard to follow because of the
    different hardware employed by each column. After
    staring at it for a while it's becoming a bit clearer,
    but at first glance it was a bit confusing.
  - Q4: Do the times from Table 3 include the inference
    time required to compute the agent state and
    observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18?


### Conclusion

  - The parallels to vision transformers are very
    interesting and help give the reader a broader
    perspective on possible applications of the current
    paper.
  - (Minor) The word "Furthermore" is repeated for two
    almost consecutive sentences. Using something like
    "additionally" to start one of the sentences can
    improve the flow of the paragraph a little.


## References

  - [hong2019patch]: Hong, Sungeun, Sungil Kang, and
    Donghyeon Cho. "Patch-level
  augmentation for object detection in aerial images."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF International Conference on
  Computer Vision Workshops. 2019.
  - [cubuk2020randaugment]: Cubuk, Ekin D., et al.
    "Randaugment: Practical automated
  data augmentation with a reduced search space."
  Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision
  and Pattern Recognition Workshops. 2020.
  - [DDAS] Liu, Aoming, et al. "Direct Differentiable
    Augmentation Search." arXiv preprint arXiv:2104.04282
    (2021).
  - [DADA] Yonggang Li, Guosheng Hu, Yongtao Wang, Timothy
    M. Hospedales, Neil Martin Robertson, and Yongxing
    Yang. DADA: differentiable automatic data augmentation.
    CoRR, abs/2003.03780, 2020.
  - [mueller2021trivialAugment]: Müller, Samuel G., and
    Frank Hutter. "TrivialAugment: Tuning-free Yet
    State-of-the-Art Data Augmentation." ICCV 2021.
  - [hendrycks2019augmix]: Hendrycks, Dan, et al. "Augmix:
    A simple data processing method to improve robustness
    and uncertainty." arXiv preprint arXiv:1912.02781
    (2019).

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a new way of looking at the classic
  task of image augmentation for classification. Instead of
  learning an image-level augmentation policy using RL, the
  proposed approach uses multi-agent RL to learn
  patch-specific augmentations. While the benefit of
  multi-agent RL is relatively small, it seems significant
  and the general idea of learning to apply different
  augmentations to different parts of the image is
  interesting and, to the best of my knowledge, new. The
  idea to apply image augmentations at a local level is the
  core contribution of this paper, in my opinion.

  While it would be interesting to see how this technique
  works on tasks which, unlike image-level classification,
  are themselves more "local", such as semantic
  segmentation or detection, overall the proposed approach
  is evaluated thoroughly.

  The writing is clear and while the authors don't
  explicitly mention an intent to publish their source
  code, it should be possible to implement the proposed
  approach just based on the description from the paper. As
  such, I recommend accepting the paper to ICLR.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Patch AutoAugment

## Summary

This paper proposes Patch AutoAugment (PAA), which extends traditional image-level data augmentation policy search to the patch level. The core idea is to divide images into a fixed grid and use multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to learn which augmentation to apply to each patch, with agents cooperating through a shared reward signal based on training loss difference. The method is evaluated across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, fine-grained recognition datasets, and object detection) and demonstrates consistent improvements over existing automated augmentation methods with lower computational cost.

## Strengths

**S1: Novel and well-motivated perspective.** The observation that image-level augmentation policies may inadvertently damage important semantic information or miss opportunities for local diversity is intuitive and well-articulated. The extension to patch-level policy search is a creative solution that addresses a genuine limitation of existing methods.

**S2: Comprehensive experimental evaluation.** The paper provides extensive experiments across diverse datasets and tasks (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, fine-grained recognition on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft, and object detection on Pascal VOC). The consistent improvements across these benchmarks demonstrate broad applicability, and the inclusion of fine-grained recognition tasks is particularly noteworthy given the potential benefits of patch-level policies for such tasks.

**S3: Computational efficiency.** The method achieves considerably lower computational cost compared to many learning-based data augmentation methods (AutoAugment, FastAutoAugment, AdvAA), as clearly demonstrated in Section 4.3. This is a practical advantage that makes the approach deployable in real-world scenarios.

**S4: Thorough ablation studies.** The paper includes meaningful ablations comparing random patch augmentation, single-agent RL variants (PSAA), and varying patch numbers (Table 7), which help justify the design choices and validate the necessity of different components.

**S5: Insightful visualizations.** The Grad-CAM and policy visualizations (Figures 3-4) provide valuable insights into what the agents learn—for instance, showing that important patches tend to prefer milder color transformations while unimportant patches favor more aggressive operations like Cutout. These visualizations aid interpretability and help understand the learned behaviors.

## Limitations

**L1: Marginal improvements on small images.** On CIFAR-10/100 (32×32 images), improvements are modest, typically ranging from 0.1-0.3% over RandAugment. While the authors acknowledge this, the paper does not deeply investigate why patch-level policies provide more benefit on larger images. This limitation raises questions about practical applicability to smaller datasets, which remain common in many domains.

**L2: Weak justification for MARL necessity.** Although the paper motivates MARL through informal arguments about "contextual relationships between patches," it lacks rigorous theoretical justification for why MARL is necessary. More critically, the comparison with single-agent RL (PSAA) shows only marginal improvements of 0.1-0.3%, which undermines the claim that multi-agent cooperation is essential. This suggests the added complexity of MARL may not be justified, and simpler spatial attention mechanisms might achieve similar results.

**L3: Limited novelty in MARL formulation.** The MARL framework applies standard multi-agent actor-critic methods (Lowe et al., 2017) with parameter sharing and centralized training/decentralized execution. The adaptation to augmentation policy search is relatively straightforward. The MARL literature distinguishes between simple reward sharing and true coordination mechanisms (e.g., value function factorization in QMIX), but this distinction is not discussed. The MARL component feels more like a direct application of existing techniques rather than a methodological innovation.

**L4: Simplistic patch division strategy.** The paper employs uniform grid-based patch division without exploring content-aware or semantic-aware partitioning. The optimal patch number varies significantly by dataset (Table 7), suggesting the fixed grid approach may be suboptimal. The fact that performance drops with very high N indicates the method requires careful hyperparameter tuning without principled guidance for practitioners.

**L5: Limited architectural diversity.** While multiple datasets are used for evaluation, only two ResNet variants are investigated for ImageNet (acknowledged in the main review). Evaluation on other modern architectures—particularly Vision Transformers, given the parallels mentioned in the conclusion—would strengthen the claims of broad applicability. While computational costs may be prohibitive, this remains a limitation.

**L6: Missing comparisons with recent baselines.** Several important recent methods are mentioned but not compared experimentally. TrivialAugment (Müller & Hutter, 2021), which the related work notes is "tuning-free" and state-of-the-art, is not included in the main experimental tables. AdvAA results are largely absent from most comparisons. Additionally, recent patch-aware augmentation methods (SaliencyMix, SnapMix, KeepAugment) are mentioned as related work but not compared experimentally, limiting understanding of how PAA relates to these orthogonal approaches.

**L7: Unclear handling of label-disturbing operations.** The interaction between patch-level policies and label-mixing operations (Mixup, CutMix) is under-explored. The paper states that patches mix with "another patch that performs the same operation," but this constraint seems artificial and could limit diversity. How agents coordinate when these operations are involved, and whether this constraint is necessary, requires clarification.

**L8: Feature extractor dependency.** The method relies on a pre-trained ImageNet ResNet-18 to extract state and observation features for the agents. While Appendix F shows non-pretrained features still work, the dependence on pre-training is not thoroughly analyzed. This could limit applicability in low-data regimes or domain-specific scenarios where ImageNet pre-training may not be available or appropriate.

## Technical Issues and Questions

**Q1: Reward function design.** The reward (Eq. 1) uses training loss difference, borrowed directly from AdvAA without novel justification. Why is loss difference preferable to validation accuracy (as used in standard AutoAugment) in the patch-based setting? Have alternative reward designs been explored?

**Q2: Parameter sharing implications.** While parameter sharing reduces model complexity, the paper does not adequately explain how agents' policies can differ when they share parameters and all receive the same global state. The only difference would be local patch observations, but this limitation in policy diversity is not discussed.

**Q3: Time horizon justification.** Setting T=1 (single action per patch) appears restrictive. Could agents benefit from sequential augmentation decisions? This design choice lacks ablation or justification.

**Q4: Computational cost measurement.** Do the times in Table 3 include inference time required to compute agent observations with the auxiliary ResNet-18? The comparison also uses different hardware (GTX 1080 Ti vs. Tesla P100/V100), making direct comparison difficult and requiring more prominent notation.

**Q5: Local vs. global tasks.** Given the more "local" nature of patch-level augmentation, have you considered more extensive evaluation on inherently local tasks such as semantic segmentation and object detection? While Pascal VOC results are included, broader evaluation on detection datasets could demonstrate where the method provides the most impact.

**Q6: Learned policy structure.** Figure 4 shows that by the end of training, "the percentage of all operations becomes almost the same," suggesting learned policies converge to near-uniform distributions. This raises questions about whether meaningful structured patterns are actually being learned or if the policies are simply regularizing toward diversity.

## Minor Issues

**Writing and Presentation:**
- Section 3.2 would read more smoothly with "Once all patches..." or "After all patches..." instead of "Until all patches..."
- "directly applying reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm" should be "algorithms"
- "In MARL configuration" should be "In the MARL configuration"
- "Exactly, we focus" (Section 4.1) should be "Specifically, we focus"
- "important levels" should be "importance levels" (Policy Visualization section)
- The word "Furthermore" is repeated in consecutive sentences in the Conclusion; using "additionally" would improve flow

**Clarity and Detail:**
- Table 3 is difficult to parse on first reading due to different hardware per column; this should be clarified upfront
- The relationship between global state and local observations in the MARL formulation needs clearer exposition
- The claim of being "first" to propose patch-level automated DA is somewhat overstated given existing patch-aware methods
- The loss function for the critic (Eq. 4) uses squared advantage, which is non-standard; typical actor-critic uses MSE loss on value estimation

**Reproducibility:**
- While hyperparameters are provided, some details are missing (e.g., critic network learning rate, discount

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes an evolution of the traditional
  pipeline of image data augmentation used to reduce ML
  model overfitting.

  Instead of applying transformations such as shear,
  rotate, CutOut, etc. at the image level, the proposed
  technique divides the images into a fixed grid and
  applies a potentially different transformation to each
  cell. The problem of selecting a transform for each cell
  is cast as a multi-agent RL (MARL) task, and the agents
  learn as the main network trains within a (multi-agent)
  Advantage Actor Critic framework.

  The agents use a shared reward mechanism, with the reward
  defined as the difference between the loss on the
  augmented sample and the original loss.

  The regular grid is fixed for a dataset and
  hyperparameters like the magnitude of the augmentations
  follow a fixed schedule; the agents only pick _which_
  augmentation to apply on a patch.

  Experiments performed on CIFAR-{10,100}, ImageNet,
  CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, and FGVC-Aircraft show
  relatively small but very consistent improvements in
  terms of image classification accuracy. Different design
  choices (MARL vs. single-agent vs. random, grid size,
  etc.) are ablated and discussed in detail.

Main Review:
## Strengths

  - S1: Ablation studies and theoretical explanations
    motivate the choice for multi-agent reinforcement
    learning reasonably well.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes an automatic data augmentation approach. Different from existing works, they proposed to augment patches in the image rather than the whole image. The approach is formulated as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem. They empirically show the effectiveness of their approach across several image classification datasets.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:
- 1.	The paper conducted a comprehensive evaluation of their approach over several datasets. They achieve gains over the compared baseline method validating the efficacy of their proposed approach.
- 2.	The approach considers patched based augmentation, which is sufficiently different/novel from existing work.

### Weaknesses:
- 3.	The motivation of the approach is somewhat lacking. For example, it is unclear why MARL on patches is much faster. I think the paper could benefit from motivating more on why MARL and what is its benefit with respect to the task. 
- 4.	The paper did not reference or compared to a key related work, “Differentiable automatic data augmentation” [A]. Based on its relevance, the paper should discuss and contrast with this work, as they also have minimal search time. Specifically, comparison in Table 2 and Table 3 would strengthen the paper.
- 5.	How is the model architecture of the actor/critic network determined? Also, what ranges of hyperparameters for MADDPG are considered? I saw the hyperparameters for the target model but not MADDPG. RL is known to be sensitive to hyperparameters, I wonder if the authors will release the code and pre-trained models for reproducibility. 
- 6.	Ablation with independent agents as a baseline would be interesting. It would further motivate the use of MADDPG and the multi-agent setting.
 
### References
[A] Li, Yonggang, et al. "Differentiable automatic data augmentation.", Proc. ECCV, 2020.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes Patch AutoAugment (PAA), a fine-grained automated data augmentation approach that extends existing image-level augmentation policy search to the patch level. The key innovation is formulating the search for optimal augmentation policies across image patches as a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problem, where each patch is controlled by an agent that learns policies based on local patch content and global image semantics. Agents cooperate via shared team rewards to achieve joint optimal augmentation. The method is evaluated on CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, fine-grained recognition datasets (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft), and object detection (Pascal VOC 2007), demonstrating improvements over state-of-the-art methods with lower computational cost.

## Strengths

1. **Novel perspective on automated augmentation**: The observation that image-level augmentation policies may miss local diversity and risk damaging semantic information is well-motivated and intuitive. Extending AutoAugment to the patch level addresses a genuine limitation of existing work, and the paper effectively illustrates this motivation with concrete examples (e.g., Figure 1).

2. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The paper provides extensive experiments across multiple datasets and tasks (CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, fine-grained classification, object detection) with consistent improvements, demonstrating broad applicability. The evaluation is thorough and the results are convincing across diverse settings.

3. **Computational efficiency**: The method achieves faster search and training times compared to AutoAugment, FastAA, and AdvAA (Table 3) through online learning, fixed schedules, and parallel patch processing, making it practical for practitioners.

4. **Insightful visualizations and ablations**: The Grad-CAM and policy visualizations (Figures 3-4) provide valuable insights—e.g., important patches preferring color transformations while unimportant patches favor aggressive operations like Cutout. The paper includes reasonable ablations comparing random patch policies, single-agent RL variants, and varying patch numbers, which strengthen the contribution.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Weak motivation and unclear benefits of MARL formulation**: While the paper motivates patch-level augmentation intuitively, the motivation for using MARL specifically is lacking. The paper claims that "contextual relationships between patches" justify the multi-agent approach, but this is not rigorously justified. More critically, the comparison with single-agent RL (PSAA in Table 7) shows only marginal improvements (0.1-0.3% on CIFAR-100, 0.1-0.2% on ImageNet), which raises fundamental questions about whether the added complexity of MARL is necessary. This undermines the core methodological contribution and suggests that simpler spatial attention mechanisms might be equally effective without the MARL overhead.

2. **Limited novelty in MARL application**: The MARL framework uses standard multi-agent actor-critic methods (Lowe et al., 2017) with parameter sharing and centralized training/decentralized execution. The adaptation to augmentation policy search is relatively straightforward. The MARL component feels incremental rather than novel—the paper applies existing techniques without significant algorithmic innovation. Furthermore, the claim that agents "cooperate" via shared rewards alone is somewhat oversimplified; the MARL literature distinguishes between reward sharing and true coordination mechanisms (e.g., value function factorization in QMIX), a distinction not adequately discussed here.

3. **Missing critical comparison with recent work**: The paper does not reference or compare against "Differentiable Automatic Data Augmentation" (Li et al., ECCV 2020), a key related work that also achieves minimal search time. This is a significant omission given the stated computational efficiency advantage. Additionally, TrivialAugment (Müller & Hutter, 2021) is mentioned but not compared experimentally, and AdvAA results are missing from most tables. These comparisons would substantially strengthen the paper and are essential for validating the claimed improvements.

4. **Marginal improvements on small images limit practical impact**: On CIFAR-10/100 (32×32 images), improvements are minimal (0.1-0.4% in many cases). The authors acknowledge this but do not deeply investigate why patch-level policies help more on larger images. Since CIFAR is a standard benchmark for automated augmentation, these modest gains limit the method's applicability and practical significance. The improvements become more meaningful on ImageNet (78.3% vs 77.6% for RandAugment), but this is still modest relative to the added complexity.

5. **Insufficient justification for design choices**: Several key design decisions lack adequate justification or exploration:
   - The reward function (Eq. 1) uses training loss difference, borrowed directly from AdvAA, but the paper does not justify why this is preferable to using validation accuracy (as in standard AutoAugment) or explore alternative reward designs.
   - The patch grid size (N=4 for CIFAR, N=16 for others) appears dataset-dependent without principled guidance. Table 7 shows performance is sensitive to N, with drops at very high values, but the explanation is superficial.
   - The time horizon T=1 (single action per patch) is restrictive and lacks justification or ablation.
   - How exactly are patches selected for label-disturbing operations like Mixup and CutMix? The paper states patches mix with "another patch that performs the same operation," but this constraint seems artificial and could unnecessarily limit diversity.

6. **Simplistic patch division strategy**: The paper uses uniform grid patches without exploring content-aware or semantic-aware partitioning. This is a significant limitation—different image regions may benefit from different levels of augmentation granularity. The observation that optimal patch numbers vary by dataset (Table 7) suggests the fixed grid approach may be suboptimal and requires careful tuning rather than providing a general solution.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Unclear technical details affecting reproducibility**: 
   - The actor/critic network architectures are not fully specified. How are these determined? 
   - MADDPG hyperparameters (learning rates, discount factor γ, network sizes) are not comprehensively documented in the main paper or appendices.
   - RL methods are known to be sensitive to hyperparameters; without full details or released code, reproducibility is questionable.
   - Table 3 computational costs use different hardware (GTX 1080 Ti vs. Tesla P100/V100), limiting direct comparability and requiring explicit acknowledgment.

2. **Limited analysis of when/why the method works**: The paper does not discuss failure cases or provide guidance on when patch-level augmentation might underperform or when image-level policies suffice. Additionally, the feature extractor relies on a pre-trained ImageNet ResNet-18 backbone. While Appendix F shows non-pretrained features still work, the dependence on pre-training is not thoroughly analyzed and could limit applicability in low-data or domain-specific scenarios.

3. **Potential issues with learned policies**: The policy visualization (Figure 4) shows that "the percentage of all operations becomes almost the same" at the end of training, suggesting learned policies converge to near-uniform distributions. This raises questions about whether meaningful, structured patterns are actually learned or whether the method essentially reverts to near-random augmentation with some task-guided bias.

4. **Statistical significance and clarity issues**:
   - While standard deviations are reported, no formal significance testing is performed to determine if improvements are statistically meaningful, especially on smaller datasets where improvements are marginal.
   - The paper claims to be "orthogonal" to region-based DA methods (Section 2.1), but this relationship could be clarified with direct empirical comparisons (e.g., SaliencyMix, SnapMix, KeepAugment are mentioned but not compared).
   - Some notation is inconsistent (e.g., switching between x_j and x without clear distinction).

5. **Technical clarity**: The loss function for the critic (Eq. 4) uses squared advantage, which is non-standard in typical actor-critic methods. Justification for this choice is missing. Additionally, the paper could better explain why patch-level policies specifically benefit fine-grained recognition tasks.

## Questions and Recommendations

1. Given that single-agent RL (PSAA) performs nearly as well as MARL, why is the multi-agent formulation necessary? This is the most critical question—a clear, empirical answer would significantly strengthen the paper.

2. Have the authors considered content-aware or saliency-guided patch division instead of uniform grids? This could address the sensitivity to patch number N shown in Table 7.

3. Why not include comparisons with Differentiable AutoAugment and TrivialAugment in the main results tables? These are important baselines that should be directly compared.

4. Can the authors provide more detailed ablations on MARL hyperpar
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes an automatic data augmentation approach. Different from existing works, they proposed to augment patches in the image rather than the whole image. The approach is formulated as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem. They empirically show the effectiveness of their approach across several image classification datasets.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
- 1.	The paper conducted a comprehensive evaluation of their approach over several datasets. They achieve gains over the compared baseline method validating the efficacy of their proposed approach.
- 2.	The approach considers patched based augmentation, which is sufficiently different/novel from existing work.

### Weaknesses:
- 3.	The motivation of the approach is somewhat lacking. For example, it is unclear why MARL on patches is much faster. I think the paper could benefit from motivating more on why MARL and what is its benefit with respect to the task. 
- 4.	The paper did not reference or compared to a key related work, “Differentiable automatic data augmentation” [A]. Based on its relevance, the paper should discuss and contrast with this work, as they also have minimal search time. Specifically, comparison in Table 2 and Table 3 would strengthen the paper.
- 5.	How is the model architecture of the actor/critic network determined? Also, what ranges of hyperparameters for MADDPG are considered? I saw the hyperparameters for the target model but not MADDPG. RL is known to be sensitive to hyperparameters, I wonder if the authors will release the code and pre-trained models for reproducibility. 
- 6.	Ablation with independent agents as a baseline would be interesting. It would further motivate the use of MADDPG and the multi-agent setting.
 
### References
[A] Li, Yonggang, et al. "Differentiable automatic data augmentation.", Proc. ECCV, 2020.

Summary Of The Review:
The main concern with the paper is with the motivation and missing reference/comparison with a key existing work. 
Currently, I recommend a weak reject.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposed a fine-grained automated data augmentation approach, Patch AutoAugment (PAA), which tries to increase diversity in local regions by divide an image into a grid of patches and search for the joint optimal augmentation policies for the patches. The proposed PAA considers the task as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem,  and adopt a multi-agent reinforcement learning
algorithm to automatically search for the optimal augmentation policies by considering the contextual relationship between the patches. They verify the proposed method on many classification and fine-grained recognition dataset(CIFAR10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet, CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars and FGVC-Aircraft). The experiments show a good result and visualization results provide some insights that the PAA  help the target network to localize more class-related cues.
reviews.main_review:
Over all, this paper is well written and technically sound, the experimental evaluation is comprehensive and the results are compelling, but I have several concerns:
1. The most important contribution of this paper in my mind is the new search space focus on image patches for data augmentation. It seems like an incremental job relative to mixup series or cutout series.
2. The proposed PPA formulate the task as a multi-agent reinforcement learning problem, and the results show it can find better policy than random, but [1] demonstrate that only use reinforcement learning can not get better policy than random baseline. The two experimental results are contrary.
3.The calimed SOTA results is inappropriate, methods with better experimental results were not compared. For example, the paper  compare with AdvAA on the  computational cost, but not coapre on the accuracy.
4.Another question is that recent work [2] with careful designed augmentation policy and advanced training strategy can get much better results, It is better to compare with it to give more insights.

[1] Cubuk E D, Zoph B, Shlens J, et al. Randaugment: Practical automated data augmentation with a reduced search space
[2] Wightman R, Touvron H, Jégou H. ResNet strikes back: An improved training procedure in timm
reviews.summary_of_the_review: According to above analysis and concerns, I suggest borderline. If the author can solve my concerns, I will consider change the score.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Patch AutoAugment

## Summary

This paper proposes Patch AutoAugment (PAA), a fine-grained automated data augmentation method that extends image-level augmentation policy search to the patch level. The key idea is to divide images into patches and formulate the search for optimal augmentation policies as a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) problem, where agents cooperatively learn patch-specific policies while considering global image semantics through a shared team reward signal. The method is evaluated comprehensively across CIFAR-10/100, ImageNet, fine-grained recognition datasets (CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, FGVC-Aircraft), and object detection (Pascal VOC 2007), demonstrating consistent improvements over existing methods with lower computational cost.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated approach**: The observation that image-level augmentation policies may miss local diversity and inadvertently damage semantic information in important regions is valid and intuitive. The extension to patch-level policies is conceptually sound and addresses a genuine limitation of prior work.

2. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The paper provides extensive experiments across diverse datasets and tasks (classification on CIFAR/ImageNet, fine-grained recognition, object detection) with consistent improvements, demonstrating broad applicability. The evaluation across multiple architectures strengthens the claims.

3. **Computational efficiency**: PAA achieves faster search and training times compared to AutoAugment, FastAA, and AdvAA (Table 3) through online learning and parallel patch processing, making it practically useful.

4. **Insightful visualizations and ablations**: The Grad-CAM and policy visualizations (Figures 3-4) provide useful interpretability—showing that important patches prefer mild color transformations while less important patches favor severe operations like Cutout. The ablation studies comparing random patch policies, single-agent RL variants, and varying patch numbers help justify design choices.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Limited novelty in MARL formulation and unclear necessity**: The MARL framework applies standard multi-agent actor-critic methods (Lowe et al., 2017) with parameter sharing and centralized training/decentralized execution—a relatively straightforward adaptation to augmentation policy search. More critically, the comparison with single-agent RL (PSAA) shows only marginal gains (0.1-0.3%), which directly contradicts the central motivation for using MARL. The paper claims that "contextual relationships between patches" justify the MARL approach, but this is not rigorously demonstrated. This raises a fundamental question: if single-agent RL achieves nearly equivalent performance, does the added complexity of MARL actually provide sufficient benefit? The paper would benefit from deeper analysis of when and why cooperation between agents matters.

2. **Marginal improvements on small images where most prior work is evaluated**: On CIFAR-10/100 (32×32 images), improvements are modest (0.1-0.4% in many cases). The authors acknowledge this limitation but do not adequately address it. This is problematic because CIFAR datasets are standard benchmarks where most automated augmentation methods are evaluated. The method's primary benefit appears to be for larger images where patch-level diversity matters more, limiting its practical impact for many common scenarios.

3. **Incremental contribution relative to existing augmentation methods**: The core novelty—applying patch-level augmentation—feels incremental relative to existing work in the mixup and cutout families. The paper does not convincingly establish why patch-level policies are fundamentally superior to region-aware augmentation methods like SaliencyMix or SnapMix, which are mentioned but not compared experimentally. The claim of being "first" to propose patch-level automated augmentation is somewhat overstated given the existence of these related patch-aware methods.

4. **Incomplete and missing comparisons with recent baselines**: 
   - TrivialAugment (Müller & Hutter, 2021) is mentioned but not compared experimentally, making it difficult to assess PAA's position relative to recent simpler alternatives.
   - AdvAA results are cited for computational cost comparison but are missing from the main accuracy tables. A direct accuracy comparison with AdvAA is essential.
   - Recent work like ResNet Strikes Back (Wightman et al., 2021) with carefully designed augmentation policies and advanced training strategies is not compared against, despite achieving substantially better results. This limits insights into whether PAA's improvements come from the method itself or simply from standard improvements in training procedures.
   - Vision transformer-specific augmentation methods are not discussed, limiting relevance to modern architectures.

## Technical Concerns

1. **Weak theoretical justification for design choices**: 
   - The reward function (Eq. 1) uses training loss difference, borrowed directly from AdvAA. The paper does not explore alternative reward designs or justify why loss difference is preferable to validation accuracy used in standard AutoAugment.
   - The claim that agents "cooperate" via shared rewards requires more careful treatment. In MARL literature, reward sharing alone does not guarantee true coordination; mechanisms like value function factorization (QMIX) are designed specifically for this. The distinction is not discussed.
   - The loss function for the critic (Eq. 4) uses squared advantage, which is non-standard. Typical actor-critic uses MSE loss on value estimation. Justification is missing.

2. **Simplistic patch division strategy**: The paper uses uniform grid patches (N=4 for CIFAR, N=16 for others) without exploring content-aware or semantic-aware partitioning. Table 7 shows that optimal patch numbers vary significantly by dataset, suggesting the fixed grid approach may be suboptimal. The paper acknowledges this in future work but should address it in the main contribution.

3. **Insufficient clarity on label-disturbing operations**: Mixup and CutMix require mixing patches from different images and modifying labels. The paper states patches mix with "another patch that performs the same operation," but this constraint seems artificial and could limit diversity. The interaction between these operations and the MARL framework is under-explored. How do agents learn to coordinate effectively when Mixup/CutMix are involved?

4. **Feature extractor dependency and generalization**: The method relies on a pre-trained ImageNet ResNet-18 backbone to extract state/observation features for agents. While Appendix F shows non-pretrained features still work, the dependence on pre-training is not thoroughly analyzed. This could significantly limit applicability in low-data regimes or domain-specific scenarios where pre-training is unavailable or suboptimal.

5. **Parameter sharing limitations**: While parameter sharing reduces MARL complexity, it may limit the ability to learn truly diverse patch-specific policies. Given that patches have different local contexts and importance levels, allowing patch-specific parameters could improve performance—but this trade-off is not explored.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Inconsistent computational cost comparisons**: Table 3 uses different hardware configurations (GTX 1080 Ti vs. Tesla P100/V100) across methods, making direct comparison problematic. This should be noted more prominently or the comparison should be standardized.

2. **Limited analysis of failure cases and sensitivity**: The paper does not discuss when patch-level augmentation might be harmful or provide principled guidance on hyperparameter selection. While Table 7 shows performance drops with very high patch numbers N, the explanation is superficial. Sensitivity analysis for other design choices (actor/critic learning rates, network architectures) is missing.

3. **Statistical significance not established**: While standard deviations are reported, no significance testing is performed to determine if improvements are statistically meaningful, particularly for smaller datasets where improvements are marginal.

4. **Visualization interpretation concerns**: Figure 4 shows that at the end of training, "the percentage of all operations becomes almost the same," suggesting learned policies converge to near-uniform distributions. This raises questions about whether meaningful patterns are actually being learned, contradicting the paper's claims about discovering interpretable augmentation strategies.

5. **Reproducibility concerns**: While hyperparameters are provided, critical details are missing:
   - Some hyperparameters (e.g., critic network learning rate, discount factor γ) are not specified.
   - Code is not provided (though this is under review).
   - The paper mentions GPU acceleration via Kornia but lacks sufficient implementation details.

6. **Writing clarity issues**: 
   - The notation switches between x_j and x without consistent distinction.
   - The claim that PAA is "orthogonal" to region-based augmentation methods is overstated.
   - The paper could better explain why patch-level policies help fine-grained recognition specifically.

## Questions for Authors

1. Given that single-agent RL (PSAA) performs nearly as well as MARL, why is the additional complexity justified? What specific evidence demonstrates that agents actually coordinate meaningfully?

2. How do you reconcile your results with prior work [1] showing that RL alone cannot consistently outperform random baselines? What makes your RL formulation different?

3

title: Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning via Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes a straightforward non-adversarially-trained imitation learning method. The goal is to avoid mode collapse and training instability that can occur in some adversarially-trained imitaiton learning methods, while still addressing the distribution shift problem that basic Behavior Cloning is subject to. 

The paper:
- Motivates a two-stage learning procedure by starting with a likelihood-based state-action distribution matching objective that would require bi-level optimization
- Presents a lower-bound of the objective in which optimization can occur serially of two components: MLE of the expert's vistation distribution, and then policy optimization over two terms, one of which is the LL of visited states under the the modelled visitation distribution, and the other is the behavior cloning loss.
- Presents a proof that it is a lower bound. 
- Proposes a concrete approach to performing this optimization problem by using a denoising conditional normalizing flow for modeling the expert's visitation distribution, where the CNF seems to be motivated because of its ability to model densities of complex distributions, and the denoising is motivated to enable stable training in situations in which the data lie in a lower-dimensional manifold. 
- Performs experiments on 10 mujoco environments that illustrate that the proposed approach achieves better mode coverage than some comparable methods, that the denoising is empirically effective for generalization, that the algorithm is more robust than GAIL to variation in learning rate, among other hyperparmaeter variations.
reviews.main_review:
Main strength
--
This paper's main strength is that it proposes a straightforward method that both conceptually and empirically satisfies important desiderata of imitation learning methods (1) address the cascading error problem of BC-based methods (2) address the instability (mode collapse) of adversarially-trained IL methods (3) avoid dependence on an interactive demonstrator (i.e. DAGGER).

Main weakness
--
The paper's main weakness is some imprecision in a few places

### Imprecision
There are some issues (a,b,c) with the proof. (a) The proof refers to component $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq 1, yet Eq. 1 has no denoted components. Is this reference correct? If so, then Eq 1. should denote the second component with an underbrace. If not, should it instead be Eq. 2 or Eq. 3? (b) The proof states "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal D_E$, $\enclose{circle}{2}$ can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$. I don't see how this is true, because $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq (3) is $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$, i.e. the state distribution is the state distribution of the policy. Again, is the reference to $\enclose{circle}{2}$ correct? These ambiguities / errors prevent me from being able to completely follow the proof. (c) It's not obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ that $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_{\pi^*}} \log P_{\pi^*}(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ for two reasons (c.i) because the sums are not normalized by the the dataset size, it would be easy to invalidate the inequality by changing the dataset sizes (c.ii) What if $\pi$ was such that $\mathcal D_\pi$ only contains $s$ at the mode of $P_{\pi^*}(s)$? Wouldn't the RHS be at least as large as the LHS, in that case?

S4.1: $P_E$ is undefined (it's mentioned that it's the expert's state distribution, but a formal definition is needed). Is it more correctly denoted $P_{\pi_E}$, to "typecheck" with the subscripting of $P$ with a policy (i.e. $P_\pi$) to denote the state visitation distribution induced by policy $\pi$?

### Other issues
- The motivation of the gating parameter ($\lambda$) seems to come from dataset size imbalance (between $\mathcal D_E$ and $\mathcal D_{\pi_{\theta_i}}$). Why can't this dataset balance be addressed by simply normalizing the losses by the size of the datasets? Can the authors comment? If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why.
- Missing related work: like the submitted paper, [A] also performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of the expert trajectories with gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions, e.g. [A] used the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy
- The description of the experiment corresponding to Figs 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) the experiment are run on.
- The introduction needs more justification for why DCNF is used in favor of other likelihood-based deep generative modeling approaches. Is continuity necessary? Is denoising necessary? Section 3 talks about the latter (C#2), perhaps incorporate some of this motivation into the introduction.

[A] Rhinehart N, McAllister R, Levine S. Deep imitative models for flexible inference, planning, and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.06544. 2018 Oct 15.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Despite some important issues with method presentation and justification, I think the paper presents a solid method and solid evidence of its utility. The paper can definitely be improved, but as it stands now, I think it's a good contribution.

Why can't this dataset balance be addressed by simply normalizing the losses by the size of the datasets? Can the authors comment? If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why.
- Missing related work: like the submitted paper, [A] also performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of the expert trajectories with gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions, e.g. [A] used the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy
- The description of the experiment corresponding to Figs 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) the experiment are run on.
- The introduction needs more justification for why DCNF is used in favor of other likelihood-based deep generative modeling approaches. Is continuity necessary? Is denoising necessary? Section 3 talks about the latter (C#2), perhaps incorporate some of this motivation into the introduction.

[A] Rhinehart N, McAllister R, Levine S. Deep imitative models for flexible inference, planning, and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.06544. 2018 Oct 15.

Summary Of The Review:
Despite some important issues with method presentation and justification, I think the paper presents a solid method and solid evidence of its utility. The paper can definitely be improved, but as it stands now, I think it's a good contribution.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes SLIL (Stabilized Likelihood-based Imitation Learning), a two-stage framework designed to address training instability and mode collapse issues in adversarially-trained imitation learning methods like GAIL. The approach first estimates the expert state distribution using a novel Denoising Continuous Normalizing Flow (DCNF), then trains a learner policy to match both the expert's policy via behavior cloning and the estimated expert state distribution. The authors evaluate SLIL on ten MuJoCo continuous control tasks and demonstrate improved mode coverage preservation, training stability, and comparable or superior performance compared to baselines including BC, GAIL, GPRIL, and DRIL.

## Strengths

The paper's primary strength is that it proposes a straightforward method that both conceptually and empirically addresses important desiderata of imitation learning: (1) addressing the cascading error problem of BC-based methods, (2) addressing the instability and mode collapse issues of adversarially-trained IL methods, and (3) avoiding dependence on an interactive demonstrator. The paper clearly identifies genuine limitations of GAIL through compelling visualizations (Figure 1) and proposes a principled alternative through likelihood-based optimization rather than adversarial training.

Theorem 4.1 provides valuable theoretical justification for the two-stage decomposition as a relaxation of the original bilevel optimization problem. The DCNF algorithm represents a creative solution to the manifold hypothesis challenge in normalizing flows, with intuitive motivation: the idea of progressively denoising data during training is well-illustrated (Figure 5) and empirically validated to improve generalization. The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, covering ten tasks with multiple evaluation perspectives including mode coverage (via KDE visualization and Earth Mover's Distance), expert state distribution estimation quality, learner policy performance, and training stability analysis. The paper demonstrates superior robustness to learning rate variations compared to GAIL, with thorough ablation studies examining the gating parameter τ and other hyperparameter sensitivity, suggesting practical advantages of the approach.

## Major Weaknesses

### Theoretical Presentation and Rigor

The paper's main weakness lies in several imprecisions and gaps in theoretical exposition. The proof of Theorem 4.1 is relegated to the appendix, and the main text provides insufficient intuition for why this relaxation is "tight" or what information is lost in the relaxation. The tightness claim requires clarification. More fundamentally, there are concerning issues with the proof itself that prevent complete verification:

(a) The proof refers to component ⊙(2) in Equation 1, yet Equation 1 has no denoted components. Either Equation 1 should have an underbrace denoting the second component, or the reference should be corrected to Equation 2 or 3.

(b) The proof states that with sufficient expert data, component ⊙(2) can be expressed as $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$. However, ⊙(2) in Equation 3 is $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \log P_E(s)$—the state distribution is the policy's state distribution, not the expert's. The claim's validity is unclear given this notation issue.

(c) The inequality $\sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_{\pi^*}} \log P_{\pi^*}(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal{D}_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ is not obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ for two reasons: (i) since the sums are not normalized by dataset size, the inequality could be invalidated by simply changing dataset sizes; (ii) if $\pi$ were such that $\mathcal{D}_\pi$ contains only states at the mode of $P_{\pi^*}(s)$, the RHS could be at least as large as the LHS.

Additionally, Section 4.1 mentions $P_E$ as the expert's state distribution but provides no formal definition. Is it more correctly denoted $P_{\pi_E}$ to maintain consistency with the subscripting convention where $P_\pi$ denotes the state visitation distribution induced by policy $\pi$? The paper also lacks convergence guarantees or sample complexity analysis for SLIL, and the connection between maximizing the two-stage objective (Eq. 3) and actually achieving good imitation is not rigorously established.

### Ad-hoc Design Choices and Hyperparameter Sensitivity

The gating function (Equation 4) appears ad-hoc and lacks theoretical justification. While empirical results suggest τ=2 works well, the paper provides no principled guidance for setting this hyperparameter across different tasks, introducing another tuning knob that contradicts claims of improved stability. The motivation for gating itself—alternating between BC and distribution matching—is presented as addressing dataset size imbalance, yet the paper does not explain why simple normalization of losses by dataset size would be insufficient. If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why explicitly.

Similarly, DCNF's noise schedule is a simple arithmetic sequence with no ablation studies justifying this choice over alternatives (geometric, exponential, or learned schedules). The initialization of σ₁ and total iterations L appear arbitrary. While the paper demonstrates robustness to learning rate changes, sensitivity to other hyperparameters (initial noise σ₁, number of DCNF iterations L) is not thoroughly analyzed. These design choices lack sufficient justification for why they represent the right approach.

### Overstated Claims and Limited Scope

The paper claims to "solve" mode collapse, but Figure 1g shows that Soft-SLIL still exhibits mode collapse, suggesting the solution may be more specific to the DCNF variant or less robust than claimed. More generally, while results show improvements in multi-modal settings, Figure 1 shows residual EMD error (non-zero in Figure 1h), and performance is comparable to GAIL in single-mode tasks (Figure 10), with advantages primarily in explicitly multi-modal settings. The claim that SLIL achieves "superior results" is somewhat overstated given this nuanced performance profile.

The experimental setup uses expert policies from TRPO with ground-truth rewards, which is somewhat artificial. Real imitation learning often involves suboptimal, noisy, or limited demonstrations with different characteristics. All experiments are confined to MuJoCo continuous control tasks; there is no evaluation on vision-based tasks, discrete action spaces, or other domains, limiting the generalizability of findings. Additionally, no statistical significance testing or confidence intervals are provided; results appear to be from single runs, which weakens the confidence in reported improvements.

### Missing Comparisons and Incomplete Analysis

The paper reports that GPRIL baseline produces "no meaningful results" without investigation, which is concerning and suggests potential implementation issues or unfair comparison. This omission is problematic given that GPRIL is a key recent baseline. The paper also lacks discussion of computational cost—DCNF requires training an ODE solver for each state and a normalizing flow before policy learning, potentially increasing wall-clock time compared to end-to-end methods or simpler approaches. No computational cost comparison with baselines is provided.

The related work section misses important context: the paper does not adequately discuss [A] Rhinehart et al. (2018), which similarly performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of expert trajectories with Gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions (e.g., [A] uses the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy). The paper could also better position itself relative to recent work on diffusion models and score-based generative models, which also address manifold issues.

### Limited Technical Novelty

While the combination is novel, the individual technical components are incremental. DCNF is essentially adding noise to data and using continuous normalizing flows (FFJORD)—the core idea of noise-based manifold handling builds on concurrent work (SoftFlow, diffusion models). The two-stage decomposition, while theoretically justified, is straightforward. The manifold hypothesis challenge (Section 3) is introduced, but the connection to why this specifically affects SLIL's LIL framework versus any normalizing flow could be clearer. Notably, the paper provides no ablation study isolating the contribution of DCNF versus the two-stage framework alone, nor does it compare against standard CNF with the two-stage approach.

## Minor Issues

### Presentation and Notation

There are several imprecisions in notation and exposition:

- The description of experiments corresponding to Figures 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) these
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a straightforward non-adversarially-trained imitation learning method. The goal is to avoid mode collapse and training instability that can occur in some adversarially-trained imitaiton learning methods, while still addressing the distribution shift problem that basic Behavior Cloning is subject to. 

The paper:
- Motivates a two-stage learning procedure by starting with a likelihood-based state-action distribution matching objective that would require bi-level optimization
- Presents a lower-bound of the objective in which optimization can occur serially of two components: MLE of the expert's vistation distribution, and then policy optimization over two terms, one of which is the LL of visited states under the the modelled visitation distribution, and the other is the behavior cloning loss.
- Presents a proof that it is a lower bound. 
- Proposes a concrete approach to performing this optimization problem by using a denoising conditional normalizing flow for modeling the expert's visitation distribution, where the CNF seems to be motivated because of its ability to model densities of complex distributions, and the denoising is motivated to enable stable training in situations in which the data lie in a lower-dimensional manifold. 
- Performs experiments on 10 mujoco environments that illustrate that the proposed approach achieves better mode coverage than some comparable methods, that the denoising is empirically effective for generalization, that the algorithm is more robust than GAIL to variation in learning rate, among other hyperparmaeter variations.

Main Review:
Main strength
--
This paper's main strength is that it proposes a straightforward method that both conceptually and empirically satisfies important desiderata of imitation learning methods (1) address the cascading error problem of BC-based methods (2) address the instability (mode collapse) of adversarially-trained IL methods (3) avoid dependence on an interactive demonstrator (i.e. DAGGER).

Main weakness
--
The paper's main weakness is some imprecision in a few places

### Imprecision
There are some issues (a,b,c) with the proof. (a) The proof refers to component $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq 1, yet Eq. 1 has no denoted components. Is this reference correct? If so, then Eq 1. should denote the second component with an underbrace. If not, should it instead be Eq. 2 or Eq. 3? (b) The proof states "with sufficient expert data $\mathcal D_E$, $\enclose{circle}{2}$ can be expressed with $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \frac{P_E(s)}{P_\pi(s)} \log P_\pi(s)$. I don't see how this is true, because $\enclose{circle}{2}$ in Eq (3) is $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$, i.e. the state distribution is the state distribution of the policy. Again, is the reference to $\enclose{circle}{2}$ correct? These ambiguities / errors prevent me from being able to completely follow the proof. (c) It's not obvious from the definition of $g(\pi, P_\pi)$ that $\sum_{s \in \mathcal D_{\pi^*}} \log P_{\pi^*}(s) \geq \max_\pi \sum_{s \in \mathcal D_\pi} \log P_E(s)$ for two reasons (c.i) because the sums are not normalized by the the dataset size, it would be easy to invalidate the inequality by changing the dataset sizes (c.ii) What if $\pi$ was such that $\mathcal D_\pi$ only contains $s$ at the mode of $P_{\pi^*}(s)$? Wouldn't the RHS be at least as large as the LHS, in that case?

S4.1: $P_E$ is undefined (it's mentioned that it's the expert's state distribution, but a formal definition is needed). Is it more correctly denoted $P_{\pi_E}$, to "typecheck" with the subscripting of $P$ with a policy (i.e. $P_\pi$) to denote the state visitation distribution induced by policy $\pi$?

### Other issues
- The motivation of the gating parameter ($\lambda$) seems to come from dataset size imbalance (between $\mathcal D_E$ and $\mathcal D_{\pi_{\theta_i}}$). Why can't this dataset balance be addressed by simply normalizing the losses by the size of the datasets? Can the authors comment? If this is insufficient, the paper needs to discuss why.
- Missing related work: like the submitted paper, [A] also performs likelihood-based imitation learning with a normalizing flow for expert distribution matching, is not adversarially trained, and uses perturbation of the expert trajectories with gaussian noise. Discussion of the differences is important to contextualize the current paper's contributions, e.g. [A] used the distribution for planning rather than to learn a final policy
- The description of the experiment corresponding to Figs 11, 12, and 13 is lacking. It's not clear what environment(s) the experiment are run on.
- The introduction needs more justification for why DCNF is used in favor of other likelihood-based deep generative modeling approaches. Is continuity necessary? Is denoising necessary? Section 3 talks about the latter (C#2), perhaps incorporate some of this motivation into the introduction.

[A] Rhinehart N, McAllister R, Levine S. Deep imitative models for flexible inference, planning, and control. arXiv preprint arXiv:1810.06544. 2018 Oct 15.

Summary Of The Review:
Despite some important issues with method presentation and justification, I think the paper presents a solid method and solid evidence of its utility. The paper can definitely be improved, but as it stands now, I think it's a good contribution.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper present a novel method for imitation learning aiming to stabilize training and mode coverage compared to existing methods. The proposed method (SLIL) extends behavioral cloning by an additional objective of maximizing a state-only reward that is defined by a density estimate (trained once beforehand) of the expert's state distribution.
For density estimation SLIL uses a continuous normalizing flow where the expert states are corrupted by Gaussian noise, where the noise-level is decreased during training. The noise is introduced to tackle the "Manifold Hypothesis Challenge", that is, the inability of normalizing flows to accurate approximate distributions that lie on a lower-dimensional manifold. 

The main contributions are:
1. A novel imitation learning method that combines the BC loss with a RL loss for reaching expert states
2. A slightly modified approach to tackle the manifold hypothesis challenge (Kim et al. [2020] used Gaussian noise where the noise-level was sampled from a uniform distribution, independently for each data set, rather than using the same noise-level for all data and annealing it during optimization)
reviews.main_review:
Strong Points
* The empirical results are good: SLIL achieves good results, no matter whether few demonstrations or many demonstrations are provided.
* The presentation is good, however, I have some suggestions below.

Weak Points
* Technical correctness (1): The main claim (Theorem 4.1) that SLIL tightly bounds the likelihood objective (LIL) is not supported. The SLIL objective differs by substituting the term $E_{p_\text{E}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\pi}(s) \big]$ with $E_{p_{\pi}(s)}\big[ \log p_{\text{E}}(s) \big]$, that is, switching the expert' and agent's state distribution. The proof has several issues: First of all, it starts with circular reasoning, by assuming that the optimal policy of the SLIL objective, $\pi^\star$,  matches the expert's state distribution. It then derives the bound (which is only valid for $\pi^\star$) that uses the equality $\max_x  f(x) + \max_x g(x) \ge \max_x \big[ f(x) + g(x) \big]$ which is not at all tight in general.
* Technical correctness (2): The SLIL objective is further modified by introducing a hyper-parameter that disables the RL objective ever $\lambda$ iterations, which effectively changes the objective being optimized without any theoretical justification.

* Novelty (1): SLIL is a hybrid between BC and Energy-Based Imitation Learning (EBIL) [2], a reference certainly missing. Like SLIL, State-only EBIL starts by estimating the expert's state distribution and uses it as a fixed reward function for imitation learning. As the SLIL loss is basically an interpolation between two prior imitation learning losses the contribution seems minor, in particular given that the objective of SLIL is not well motivated (Technical correctness (1)). It is also worth noting that AIRL uses noise contrastive estimation to estimate the expert policy and uses this density estimate directly as reward for MaxEnt-RL, which is similar to the 2nd term of the SLIL objective. The differences are thus that AIRL ues MaxEnt-RL instead of RL, $r=\log p_E(a|s)$ instead of $r=\log p_E(s)$, and NCE with an EBM for density estimation rather than maximum likelihood with normalizing flows. 

* Novelty (2): The "Denoising continuous normalizing flow" seems to be a minor variant of Softflow [Kim et al. 2020]. Furthermore, this minor contribution is not evaluated at all (neither Softflow, nor any other methods for handling the manifold hypothesis challenge were compared). 

* Evaluation:  The improved stability compared to GAIL was not demonstrated. Please add learning curves for the data of Fig. 10 to the Appendix (choosing a suitable number of demonstrations (e..g. 5) for each experiment).

Additional Comments
* It is not clear why the hyper-parameter lambda was introduced in favor of a fixed coefficient that trades off the two objectives, which would at least maintain a fixed objective function. 
* Much of 4.2 seems to be related work. I think it would make more sense to put it before the "Method" section to make the contributions more clear. In exchange, the problem formulation of LIL could be put under method, since it is introduced to "derive" the SLIL objective.
* Algorithmbox 1 is almost useless, because it mainly shows that we have an iterative training loop, but doesn't really show what happens at each iteration.
* End of page 5. The definition of $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s}|s)$ looks like  $P_{E,\sigma}(\tilde{s},s)$ to me.

References:
[1] Liu, R., Gao, J., Zhang, J., Meng, D., & Lin, Z. (2021). Investigating bi-level optimization for learning and vision from a unified perspective: A survey and beyond. arXiv preprint arXiv:2101.11517.

[2] Liu, M., He, T., Xu, M., & Zhang, W. (2020). Energy-based imitation learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2004.09395.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The contributions seem rather incremental and the claims are not well-supported.
I still gave a relatively high recommendation, because the results are fine and the overall ideas of 1) tackling covariance shift of BC by using an RL objective to match the expert state distribution, and 2) estimating the expert state distribution once instead of iteratively based on NCE with adapted noise to improve stability, are reasonable.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper present a novel method for imitation learning aiming to stabilize training and mode coverage compared to existing methods. The proposed method (SLIL) extends behavioral cloning by an additional objective of maximizing a state-only reward that is defined by a density estimate (trained once beforehand) of the expert's state distribution.
